commentBody:
“The New York Times crossword puzzles will no longer feature wordplay,” said Games crossword spokesperson Aria DiOreo.  “It has grown out of date, worn, and will be phased out.”  

The reaction was swift:
“We’re in treble,” said a chorus teacher.
“It’s about tine,” said a resident of Grand Forks.
“Don’t shout it, please,” said someone, “but I’d like some confirmention.”
“I am in great dis-tress,” said someone else who just shaved their head in protest.
“Don’t know what to think of this,” said a see-saw.
“They never get it right,” snorted Mr. Ed.
“Too busy working my NFL football muscles climbing rungs,” said a ladder-day Saint.
“Poppycock!” said a chick hatchling seeing its daddy for the first time.

 (It’s good to start out April foolish…)
Mildly infuriating indeed. Is this just an anti-joke?
Took me a while to understand the theme, and I was a little let down when I did - but knowing it's an April Fool's joke has me appreciating it a lot more!
Come on, mini.   “Geez” is an inappropriate nickname for someone a bunch of us worship
Monday easy.  Yesterday’s puzzle should have been today’s.

You know, I pretty much like all the puzzles.
Including this one. (“Ohhh, part one of “QUIP”, I get it!”)

But I’m sad to have been deprived of a depraved April Fool trick. We’ve had some doozies over the years.

Oh well, maybe someone will play a trick on me tomorrow. Here’s hoping!
I didn't realize until reading this column that this was an April Fool's Day puzzle.  And a nice one it was!  A gentle poke at crossword convention that was just right for the day.
Even after reading the wordplay I still feel like I’m missing something here… hmm?

Well, okay then. Here's that Monday puzzle many people were crying for on Monday. And it comes with a theme that you not only don't need to fill in the grid, but you may not want to have known when you discover it. I guess that's fair for an April Fool's Day puzzle. 
Thanks for your efforts, Jess and Amie.

Puzzled by the non-quip nature of the theme I had to read Wordplay to learn that there was actually a (very strained) logic to the whole thing.  I like a challenging puzzle, but a theme this esoteric that is basically irrelevant to the solve doesn’t move me.  I did like the clue for DOGTOY and the mention of Phil OCHS, perhaps the most earnest of the 60’s folk/protest singers, who sadly isn’t marching anymore, having died at the tender age of 36.

Pretty fun fairly easy Wednesday puzzle.  I'm still confused by the theme, even with the 'April Fools' explanation it seems like a reach.

I liked the approach to clues in general, to me the best clues are the ones that are a little misleading but make sense when you look at them in a different way.  Like "DOG TOY" as the bone that squeaks.

I've never heard of a DOWDY to the point when I had 'Something's amiss' I had to google it to make sure it was a real thing.  (Turned out I had ROUSE instead of ROUST).

While this was a fine puzzle, it didn't work for me as an appropriate April 1 puzzle. The gimmick was gimmicky, but not April-Fools-y. Here's a list of April 1 puzzles. Some of them are much better tricks for the first day of the fourth month. The 2016 one sticks out for me: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Thumbs/?select=day&month=4&day=1
OK, I get it. Solving on my phone, I did not see that the Q was circled, so it made no sense at all to me till I read Wordplay. Feeling a little let down.
The Midi-puzzle was...cute.  April 1, and all that.

Well, now I have a 100,000,000 day streak...
.
.
.
...in binary
I loved it! Loved the wordplay (love meta phrases), loved the twist on the “part 1”-type clues. Cute and fun!
Near as I can tell, the prank was that the theme is utter nonsense and you just fill in the phrases from the crossing letters.

"Was that a really important quotation?"
"No, it was just a quip on the radar."

("Well, I, 4/1, enjoyed it.")

What to say? I hate to burst the bubble of these two constructors who put a lot of time, thought and heart into this. This would have been a fine Monday puzzle on an average day. I suppose it was put on Wednesday because the editors thought it was April Foolsworthy. But it just really wasn’t. Cute idea, yes, gave you a second of pause when you had to figure out why the quip wasn’t a quip, but just a second. Oh and let’s circle the letters just in case there was a remote chance that we might actually fool someone, we wouldn’t want that. And in the meantime how many brilliant April Fools tricks have we seen through the years— is there nothing trickier that had been submitted? (Even if it were a little trickier than the average Wednesday?) And maybe a misleading or tricky clue  somewhere in the grid? Anywhere?
I think by any estimation both Monday and Tuesday were more engaging and Wednesday worthy than today.
I’m sorry in the spirit of kindness I really debated posting this, I don’t want to hurt the constructors or any poster who really liked it. But I think I can at least be honest, and if I had created and  submitted this and it had been posted with those clues on an April Fools Wednesday I would be very disappointed and would not be hurt by someone saying so.
Somewhere Merl Reagle is chuckling.

I gotta say… 

I only like crosswords that contain the word EEL.

EEL = A MORAY

No EEL, no love from me.

Today’s puzzle had no EEL. I was bereft. 

So I changed 41A from ANNE to EELS. 

Dearest puzzle editors, I shouldn’t need to add the EELS to the puzzles myself! Thank you for your attention to this shocking matter.

A bit disappointed and hopes deflated for an April Fool's day trick in the puzzle. The puzzle itself was enjoyable, but that was about it. 

Just hoping that the Artemis II launch will not turn into an April Fool's trick, and that the weather will be clear enough that I can see it from the sidewalk in back of my apartment building. The launch site is 64 miles ENE from my location, but we can usually see the rocket flames ascending from Cape Canaveral when there's a launch.
The puzzle was so easy and the theme so obscure! Even with a explanation of the consfructors' intent, I did not find the idea or the result "brilliant."
otoe! as the prophechy foretold

I was just a little disappointed that GEEZ was clued GGGG and WISE came from YYYY ... But ARSE didn't get the same treatment? 

Commit to the bit.

Okay, I get it now but I feel like the explanation makes it sound harder than it is. We were just meant to spell out QUIP and every answer was a clue for where all four “parts” of the word were located. I think it was a fun April Fools prank because probably very few people got it but once you come here it makes sense.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: Just when I thought I'd give Guardian cryptograms another try after being utterly meh'ed by them the first time a year ago, along comes this "quip" to warn me off.

I have to assume the puzzle was submitted with no expectation of circled letters, because with them it becomes… redundant? 

Or maybe that’s the joke. “You wanted an April Fool’s prank? How about a puzzle you don’t have to solve because we did it for you! And then circled the answers! Hahahaha!”

I don’t get it.
Worst puzzle in years.  Inclusion of Q, U, I, and P is boring, not a trick.

And now I can't get this out of my head: "Quick start? Young at heart. Second in line? Pay up front!" I'm looking forward to throwing that into a conversation with a smile and a wink. It sure sounds like it should mean something!
I enjoyed the puzzle.  Thank you.
Did anybody else see contestant Kimberly on Jeopardy March 31st? She said she lost a 7, 000 NYT crossword streak due to a glitch. 😮

I liked the puzzle. I needed the blog to get the wordplay for each letter of QUIP, but that didn't affect my enjoyment. I detest April Fool's gags, but this didn't read like one. Then again, based on the other comments, I seem to approach these puzzles very differently. Paragraphs just to say...you didn't like it. Wild.

Today's puzzle seemed more like a Monday. Monday seemed more like a Tuesday. Tuesday seemed more like a Wednesday. 
I don't know what this world coming to.
Maybe a week-long April Fool's joke?

I just solved the puzzle, ignoring that there was supposed be a theme or something, and it went quickly for a Wednesday, which was okay by me because I'm feeling too ragged to deal with cleverness unless it's necessary for a solve, and it wasn't. An inside joke?  Maybe they should have let it out a little more. 

Anyway, thanks for your time and trouble, Jess and Amie.  It was probably fun to construct, so it had some good to it after all.
What a bizarre combination of boringly direct, Monday clues and obscure pseudo-theme... If ever a puzzle deserved a 1/10 review, this was it.
Should’ve kept it for April 1st 2030
Wut

Randomly enough, I tried to submit a puzzle my first NYT crossword for today that had sorta the same theme. "X part of a quote about April Fools" where the themers were: 

First: THESISSTATEMENT 
Middle: BODYPARAGRAPH
Last: CONCLUDINGWORDS 

It probably could have used some work (it was the first puzzle I submitted since 2019, and I wrote that one years earlier when I was a teenager), but it's kind of a small world coincidence!

Driving 1,207 miles to get my youngest 
settled in to his first med school apartment,
I got three straight days of hanging out
with by boy.

He’s magic, btw.

We used Waze for most of it,
and somewhere in the final 100 miles
he noticed he could customize the voice
to his own.

As I drove,  I listened to
one of my favorite voices on earth
record the phrases into my phone-

“…in half a mile…”
“…turn right at the next light…”
And instead of saying that 
I’d “arrived at my destination”, 
he said,
“Now get your smelly feet out of the car.”
There’s an audible chuckle as he says it.

He still lives in Florida.
Now a resident.
He’s not moving back.

But for five years
his mamma has used Waze
to drive to the grocery store,
tool around town…

just to hear his voice.

And to his silly voice tell me
to get my smelly feet out of the car.
7 minute Wednesday is about half my average time. Happy to solve it without hints, but could have been a tad harder. Thanks for a good time anyway.
This puzzle was bad. Very bad. The theme was bad. Why was this published?
Ho hum. I was looking forward to being fooled, and finding out that’s the joke - no fooling - is a bummer.
Weak theme and not all that clever. Solved in one of the fastest Wednesdays I’ve ever done. Really NYT?

OHNEAT! A Wednesday PB!

I just skipped over the themers until I had a lot of crosses. Not a hard one, but fun.

Thanks!
I'm disappointed in this one after past April Fools' puzzles. Of course, it's hard to live up to the expectations you've set year after year. I just didn't find this one all that funny.

Finished the grid without really getting the gimmick. The "Quote" just aint funny. or much or anything. Anticlimatic I guess. Best part of the puzzle was the shout out to Phil Ochs, who died too young and would have had a lot to say (and sing) about our modern day political situation

Didn't get the theme at all until I read the column :') Loved this one.

In my bouldering gym, route difficulty is indicated using a color-coded circuit grading system. A route with a yellow tag could be anywhere from a V1 to a V4 (that's about a Monday to Wednesday/Thursday difficulty range in the average gym). I love this system in part because certain routes can vary in difficulty from person to person. For example, a tall climber who loves dynamic movements will climb differently than a climber with small hands who prefers slow, controlled movement. Each will have their own assessment of route difficulty based on their personal strengths. 

When I do a puzzle, especially on a holiday, I usually approach it under the assumption that its perceived difficulty may fall within a day or two of today's expected difficulty. Some Mondays kill me (the Latin from last week or so?? woof.) and sometimes I fly through a Friday. Idk. I really enjoyed this one, even if I did finish it pretty early for a Wednesday. I like when the theme is a thinker bc it trains me to think about clues differently in the future. I also really liked all the references to other clues in the puzzle. Yaay Wednesday <3

Props to Jess and Amie for sticking with this for two years to get it right.  The rebel in me likes how it parodies quip puzzles because the theme answers don’t form a cogent saying or punchline.

I especially liked SECOND IN LINE.  The other theme answers directly tell where the circled letter is in that answer, but SECOND IN LINE is more subtle.  You gotta think a little harder to figure it out, as it’s not the second letter in that answer.

I liked seeing the sing-song BOGIE / DOWDY / INDIE / ASHY / VEGGIE – try saying that five times fast.  I loved [Bone that squeaks, maybe], which had me cataloging human bones (and I think I actually have one that squeaks) – lovely misdirect.  Also, after filling in CASH for [Alternative to check or card], lovely to see VISA as the next answer.

CRYPT, ROUST, and TACIT are words I love.

So, much to like for me.  This puzzle had a sweet feel, and that’s a lovely way to start the day.  Thank you, Jess and Amie!
I didn't much get the theme, even with the Wordplay, but the puzzle was still a fun solve.  Thank you!

I was going to come here to express my disappointment, but so many have beaten me to the punch on this, that I decided to keep it to myself.  I hate to pile on.  I know that the constructors (and editors?) are working hard to provide us with amusement.

And I was going to leave without commenting, when I figured out why I was disappointed.  So I had to share my thoughts.

The four themers are essentially unclued.  You'd need to know the gimmick in advance in order to have some clue, but knowing the gimmick in advance would ruin the gimmick.
And because the themers are unclued, the crosses have to be Monday-level easy.  But that's not fun on a Wednesday.
And then we're supposed to get some satisfaction from realizing that the themers are actually cryptic-style clues to tell you which letter is being highlighted.  But we already have the circles; we know which letter is being highlighted.  So the cryptic clues feel superfluous.
If they were really good, we wouldn't need the circles, but then we'd need some sort of revealer to tell us to look at the themers as cryptic clues.
So, the result:  kinda disappointing.

And had the constructors and editors found a way to deal with all this?  It still wouldn't have been April Fools' level tricksy.

[Sigh]
Awesome! This got me good. I was aware it's April Fools Day, and am usually a bit more wary on this day... but I fell for this 100%. 'I don't get it...' I thought. Well played! 🤣

Well, obviously: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zDA5ZDarEE4&list=RDzDA5ZDarEE4&start_radio=1 Less obviously: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ru2tsT32pHA&list=RDRu2tsT32pHA&start_radio=1 (Did people still smoke in music videos in 1982?) Trivia note: Humphrey Bogart and Lauren Bacall were married at Malabar Farm, an experimental farm just east of Mansfield, Ohio. It is now a state park. The puzzle? 12D.
Clued like a Monday or Tuesday.

I know this is supposed to be a parody of a "quip puzzle," and I know that Mr. Shortz famously belittles them, and they appear seldom--as in "never"--in the NYT XW; but I have always enjoyed a good "quip puzzle." Short poems of Ogden Nash make for good ones!

Kinda like cryptic crossword 101, with the clue to the theme in the answer itself. Clever! https://www.theguardian.com/crosswords/crossword-blog/2012/jan/27/cryptic-crosswords-for-beginners-initial-letters

"Sure, you have to ignore the meanings of the words that contain the circled letters in order to appreciate the theme in toto. But what is an April Fool’s prank if not mildly infuriating?"

Well...
Easy fill, didn't get the theme until I read the column. Truly one of the Wednesdays of all time

I found the theme clues quite opaque and I didn't get the meaning of it all. I'm really not good with themes.

The slang for the Happy Meal chain (glad they didn't use 'restaurant' because that would imply food) is 'Maccies' in the UK, so I found out after research. I didn't know the US one. 

I entered SASS immediately, it's been used quite a bit lately and I've finally learnt it; still slow with GOTTA, getting there with DEN being a room and IHOP being pancakes (flapjacks?). Do I need to remember DOWDY?
If you’re itching for some really good April Fools themes: https://tinyurl.com/yp4v6nss

Some puzzles are more fun to create than solve. This one seems to me to fall in that category. I usually don't start doing the puzzles until Thursday, but April Fool's Day seemed like it  might be fun. Having last done Sunday's wonderful puzzle, however, this one left me cold.

(G)OOD one!
T(O)o cleaver by half!
BU(T) finally,
WE(M)ET in the middle.
TH(E) end.
Do people really call MacDo's MickeyD's?
This just made me miss Frank Longo's crosswords.

Wow was I wrong: I thought "pay up front" referred to "cash" because it was a way to pay at the top of the puzzle; then second in line was again "cash" because it was the second word in the first line;  quick start could be "cash" because it was in the beginning; young at heart referred to a word at the heart of the puzzle, which was "green" and green also means young and cash. I thought its April Fools Day and Ogden Nash is mentioned who is known for writing silly rhymes. So I googled Ogden Nash quips about cash, and came across one of his famous poems called "Bankers are Just Like Everybody Else, Except Richer" and interestingly it has a non-rhyming pattern which this quip in the puzzle is. After reading the very interesting poem, I think this cant be it and come to read the answer and I think HUH?  So I decided this was the answer: a meta for tax time, and easter because I really ended up down a rabbit hole and in pure April Fools mode, the jokes on me! BUT, that poem by Nash, now that is some pure wisdom for this time.

Nice Wednesday puzzle with a different kind of trick. Took me a while to tumble to the theme but that just made for an enjoyable solve. Puzzle finds today were inspired by an old joke popping into my head. Anyway - first one was a Sunday from May 15, 2016 by Patrick Berry with the title: "Exhibit A." Some theme answers: AGGRAVATEDASALT APATCHYHELICOPTER YOUCANTWINAMALL THATSAMORAY And there were a couple of others. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/15/2016&g=46&d=A I'll put my other puzzle finds in a reply. ...

Dear fellow NYT crossword fans:
1: Today is April 1st, also known as April Fools Day
2: Quoting the Cambridge Dictionary, April 1 is 'when people play tricks on each other and try to make people believe things that are not true.”
3: This puzzle seems to have successfully made us all believe that we were solving for a witty 4-line quip. After filling in Parts 1 and 2, many of us probably expected it to rhyme. (I did!)
4: In the end, we were had! It's a prank! There is no witty quip, just circled letters spelling out 'quip'.
5: Be honest: did you sit there, trying to figure out why this seemingly random set of phrases was a quip? I did! 
6: We fell for the prank. April Fools Day in the US is not meant to be a day for funny jokes or stories or even witty quips. It's a day to trick people, and this puzzle seems to have achieved this to an amazing degree.
Happy April Fools Day!

I usually enjoy being tricked, but I think I was too sleepy today. The theme just made me grumpy. Not the constructors' fault; I've been super grumpy lately and they got caught in the cross(word)fire.

I did like seeing MICKEY D'S, though. I never eat there, on account of it's disgusting, but that nickname always makes me smile for some reason.
Oh brother 🙄

I often have my opening sentence written in my head well before I come here to write it and often before I've finished the puzzle.  And today my opening line was going to be: "If memory serves, we all hate this variety of puzzle, don't we?"  I was referring to quip puzzles, or as they are better known, quote puzzles.  

But I can't use that opening line because this is NOT a quip puzzle.  QUICK START/YOUNG AT HEART/SECOND IN LINE/PAY UP FRONT is neither a quip nor a quotation.  Why is it clued as though it were??

Instead, it's a "look at which letter is in the tiny little circle and where it is placed in the phrase and how it relates to the phrase.  Illustrates it, even.

Which is fine.  Just don't call it a quip.  And if you insist on calling it a quip, for heaven sake don't label it "part 1" and "part 2".

As to everything else, first of all I found this puzzle -- with its SOY and VEGGIE clues -- to be shameless propaganda for meat substitutes.  I regard non-meat meat to be non-food food.  I am always open to eating real VEGGIES, but I refuse to eat pretend meat.

Also -- is it DOWDY and MICKY D'S?  That was my guess.  I don't know the restaurant chain (do we even have them in NYC?) and I never heard of the dessert.  I've heard of Apple Brown Betty, but not of Apple Pan DOWDY.  If DOWDY is right, that is.
They got me good!  I don't mind.
April fools! It's a Monday puzzle on Wednesday!
Satisfying solve, but I’m really not sure I get the quip? Joke? Fool?
Today’s Mini: I wish the clue for 8A had been “R R R R R”. The puzzle needed to be even sillier than it was.

I turned off my timer a long time ago, but I am certain this was the fastest solve of any day in any week for me.  There was no need to know the theme, the meaning of the circled letters...just a very, very quick run through easy clues and obvious answers.  Grocery store crossword book material.

I thought today would be my fastest time for a Wednesday, but alas, it wasn't. Still, pretty close. It was fun, though. One thing I love about crosswords is that it's okay to talk out loud to myself.

Actually, in 2026, you can talk out loud to yourself anywhere and no one thinks anything of it. I remember, around the turn of this century, when it was still odd to see people talking to themselves as they walked down the streets of NYC. Just a year or two later, everyone was doing it, as mobile phones became more prolific.

Where am I going with this? Nowhere...time to get to the Bonus Puzzle. Should be a doozy today. Have a fun and careful April 1!
As a kid,on  April 1st I’d put paper in toe of my dad’s shoes. He always acted surprised. He was a great dad.

In some locations and countries, MICKEY D'S will, in fact offer a [burger which lacks meat]:--the McPlant®, but also, in India the McSpicy Paneer® and the McAloo Tikka®. The only Mickey D's I eat at regularly is one off I-75, just north of Saginaw, MI--very clean, very friendly. I will have to ask for a McPlant the next time I stop. Mickey D's founder Ray Kroc was concerned that, in markets with a large Catholic clientele, hamburger sales dropped during Lent. So he wondered whether a non-(red)-meat-based sandwich would sell. Kroc envisioned a McHawaiian--basically a slice of grilled pineapple on a hamburger bun, with lettuce and tomato. But his friend, and multiple franchise owner, Louis Groen suggested the Filet-o-Fish®. Initially, Kroc was against the idea--would make the restaurants smelly. So the two got into a friendly competition for sales, and you can see whose idea has the "®" after it! With his money, Groen bought a pretty chunk of property in Northern Lower Michigan, in Johannesburg, just east of Gaylord. It is now a nature preserve: https://www.groennaturepreserve.com/

I remember hearing the term "apple pan dowdy" in a song by June Christy. If you are interested, https://shorturl.at/ml3jV I first heard tbe song on a local jazz program on our NPR about June Christy, actually. Stan Kenton-- meh.
Today's Midi had a better April Fool's theme.  I'm really enjoying the Midis!

I'd like to think it was not Jess and Amie's idea to circle the letters spelling out QUIP.  

How long would it have taken me to figure out that I was being pranked?

How would I have rationalized the "QUIP" and tried to make it make sense?

Now, *that* would have been funny, and I'm giving Jess and Amie the credit, because I'm betting that editing put in the circles.

Really enjoyed this one! Satisfying "aha" moment realizing that the theme entries were meta references to their own circled letters, and fun to try and reverse-engineer that process. I wasn't familair with the term "quip puzzle", so the "prank" aspect was lost on me, but still very neat!

All puzzles solved for today. For me, the most creative puzzle was the mini by Sam Ezersky…loved it. My fave puzzle of the day was Connections…if you haven’t looked at it, do take a moment. The sudoku puzzles were good today…the medium was a great one for new folks and the hard was harder…love all three. Time to go feed the cats…again.
" Grime doesn't pay", said the forlorn janitor.
Apple Pan Betty is more familiar to me than, Apple Pan Dowdy, so that cost me some time when I had to retract it.

I enjoyed today’s puzzle, from BOGIE thru ATTN.  A few groaners but hey that is part of the fun!

And I learned the difference between apple pan Betty and apple pan DOWDY.  That alone puts the puzzle up in the 95th percentile.  

And add the terrific photo Sam Corbin found, also Francis quip about the 10 types of people.

The rest of the news in this world is a fresh he** daily, and our internet has become a predatory wasteland.  

But the NYT crossword remains a haven of jaunty goofy amusement.  Thanks for that!
I finished the puzzle quickly but even after reading the explanation, don't understand the joke.
Disappointed by today's puzzle? Go try this month's bonus puzzle, which also dropped today, and touch grass.
Themeless Wednesday for me. The "quip" was an unknown for me. Will need to read the column and comments to better understand this puzzle. Solved it easily enough, but clearly I've missed something.

The nomenclature of baked fruit desserts is confusing, and often contradictory or inconsistent. Here's a brief rundown:
Crisps--fruit below, crumbs on top
Cobblers--fruit below, sweet biscuit dough on top
Buckles--biscuit or dense cake batter below, fruit on top, may crumbs on top of that.
Brown Betties--crumbs below and on top, fruit in middle.
Pandowdy--(per *Joy of Cooking*) fruit below and on top, pastry dough in middle(?) although perhaps @BA could clarify.

There are also Slumps and Grunts, not to mention all those European interlopers: Clafoutis (fruit in pancake-y batter), various types of Galettes and Crostate, and, of course, the Queen of all, Tarte des Demoiselles Tatin!
An ok Wednesday puzzle, but that's the worst "quip" (if it even is a quip) I've seen in 35 years of working the NYT puzzle.
I didn’t get the quip—I had to come here and read the explanation. But that is not a quip, it is a collection of dad jokes!  (Okay, yes, it’s clever—and maybe funny!)
The YOUNG AT HEART construction is a bridge too far IMO. Someone was trying too hard with that.

I too did not get the significance of the placement of the circled letters relative to the answers until reading the commentary. And I’m still looking for a quip. Thematically, this one is a fail for me, and that’s a rarity.
After solving and then reading the column, my assessment of this puzzle, as an April Fools' trick, is 12D. (Either the clue or the answer.)
I was kinda hoping every answer in today's puzzle would be OREO, but that's probably just me. Nary an Oreo in sight!

A fine one but the positional significance of the Q-U-I-P eluded me. Thanks Sam. Our local weekly paper ran an April Fools Day front page. Some sample headlines: "Ocean City to Replenish Beaches with Kitty Litter" Cape May Woman says 15 Minute Drive 'Just Too Much'" https://www.capemaycountyherald.com/

A spring poem for April 1st…made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ awaken my jellyfish
my cool cheek ramona
awaken! the golly bone squeaks!
leave a word undisturbed
the pillowcases quip and ‘quip
the world ways wow
d/ hear me out listen how
i insect the underground
so usted can
with toe pads toodle
over moments of meh
protest the nervous mainstream
and a/ roust the bogie tree frog green

Sad to see read the first few comments and see that some really didn't like this puzzle. I found it fun and a bit challenging and a terrific April Fools Day gift. I did it last night but didn't get the happy music. So after finishing all the other puzzles I do every day (Mini, Midi, Wordle, Connections, Strands) I went back to this one and found my mistake. Now I can't remember what that mistake was, but no matter. It was definitely a fun puzzle day for me and these days I'll take any fun I can get. I LOVED Connections. It took a lot of head-scratching and a few wrong tries, but victory was sweet.
Thanks to all the constructors, editors, and of course, Sam. 
It's raining here, and we really needed it. I'm going to put my rain gear on and venture out for my daily hill walk among the cedars and the beautifully blooming dogwoods.
Stay safe everyone :-)

And one more puzzle find. A Sunday from April 7, 1991 by Valerie Meunier with the title: "Cross-Examination" A couple of clue and answer examples: "Kalmar-Ruby Question" WHOSSORRYNOW "A Capulet's question" WHATSINANAME And some other theme answers: WHATSUPDOC HOWAMIDOING WHOSTHERE WHOISSILVIA PARLEZVOUS AINTSHESWEET WHATSCOOKING WHATSMYLINE HOWNOWBROWNCOW WHOSONFIRST HOWDOILOVETHEE WHATSMYLINE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=4/7/1991&g=121&d=A ....
Yogi Berra would like Alina's Midi.

The puzzle was fine, and the best part was Sam’s link to the history of “a-prefixing”—thank you, Sam. 
And also the reminder in a comment from Margaret about the “shoo fly pie, apple pan dowdy” song.  Thank you, Margaret.
Proof again for me just how enriching doing a crossword puzzle can be.

The puzzle was fun and I confess that even after I solved it, I was still scratching my head wondering, "Did I miss something?" Naturally, I came directly to Wordplay to find out.

The puzzle author's comment, "Get it? The puzzle is ‘illy’ quipped??? We’re hilarious." made me laugh out loud.

Thanks to Sam for the word "a-prefixing"! I always love a new  linguistic tidbit in my day.

The puzzle was fine, but the theme was really very weird. I read the blog to get the thing and was like "Oh. Okay." but I do not think this deserved a Wednesday slot. it should have been a Monday or, at best, a Tuesday. The midi today did a better prank on us by invoking DEJA VU.

I'M afraid that, for mE, tHis puzzle was. . . well. . . meh.

For an April Fools' puzzle, I was hoping for the equivalent of spaghetti growing on trees, or an erupting dormant volcano. But no such luck. Ah well, there's always next year, with April Fools' Day on a Thursday. (Maybe more of a foolish challenge!)

I did like the illustration, though.

And one more puzzle find. A Sunday from April 1(!) 1984 by Frances Hansen with the title "!Pu Smottob". A couple of theme answers: LOOFLIRPAEHTSEMOCEREH YAWWYMNOMIEMROFTIAWUOY ELURDROWSSORCAKAERBOT And there was one more. Oh, and those were all across answers. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=4/1/1984&g=23&d=A ....
Here's a comment for you --- I did them both and the midi was cleverer and more fun.

I don't comment much, but the crossword itself was fine. The theme is a complete dud. I had to read the column once, then the comments, then go back to the column again to get what the "theme" is. 

Just make a crossword lol

I did figure it out without the column, but the lack of any quippishness in the theme entries left me with a shrug. I liked the cryptic nature of the clues but we see those in crosswords all the time now.

Anyway. It was obviously hard to pull off, and the puzzle felt a little April Foolish, sort of.
Monday took me a Wednesday amount of time to solve this week, so I guess it's only fitting that Wednesday took me a Monday amount of time.
I read the column, I don’t get the quip thing. Is it just that the answers are random phrases that happen to contain one of the letters from quip?
Where can I find the NYT bonus crossword puzzles?

Boy, this was a rough one. Setting aside the theme, this has some of the worst fill I have seen in a long time. A particular lowlight are the ugly plurals: tams, ETAs, RTEs, Ikeas, OTs, and so on (Ikeas in particular)

There's also a lot of just generally lazy, supremely trite fill overall: Ashe (and ashy), Otoe, ores, Anti/Ante, and ahas. I'm surprised Aer and Oreo didn't make an appearance.

But by far the worst offense is an answer clued "how __ you?"

Anyway, I guess I got pranked by spending my time doing a terrible puzzle.

I solved this in half my average time for a Wednesday, but until I read the column and well over half the comments, I didn’t get it. Let me see if I understand: the quip was not really a quip in order to make fun of puzzles that use quips? And that’s the “joke”? Sadly, if the joke requires an explanation … well, you know. I feel bad for the constructors though. It sucks when a joke falls flat. Still, kudos for 22A [Bone that squeaks, maybe]. I love that! 🦴
Nicely done for an April fool's puzzle.
In the spirit of most April fools, underwhelming

Apparently I am tempted more than I am BLAMEd, since I tried tempt at 1A  immediately and felt very confident in it. My sweet temptation only lasted as long as 14A though, of course. 

Oh well, if I'm never tempted I guess I can't be blamed. 

35A has a cute clue for SUE--Go a-courtin'.

And, I guess that's all IGOTTASAY. TATA!

sorry if this is a repeat. In today's GAMEPLAY there's a link to an article about April 1 puzzles over the years, with links to five puzzles. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/01/crosswords/april-fools-best-puzzles.html?nl=gameplay&segment_id=217563

I remember one afternoon in ‘46 it was Louis B Mayer, Alan Ladd, Bogie, Abe Grundleton the renowned orchestrator, and me grabbing lunch at the Warners commissary. In those days Bogie had a lunchtime ritual. Every day at precisely 12:30 he would roll up his sleeves and start sniffing at the crotch of his elbow crease until his nose started to bleed. At this point someone, usually Bacall or one of his handlers would gently take him by the shoulders and say, “Bogie, it’s time for lunch.”  To which he would reply, “Nothin doin!” and proceed to gnaw at his own forearm flesh until the studio medical unit arrived, strapped him to a gurney and rolled him to the commissary. So anyway, there we all were eating our tuna on toast with glasses of milk and good ol’ Bogie sitting there with his open arm wounds, and suddenly Grundleton, seemingly out of nowhere, starts braying like a mule.  And when I say braying like a mule, I mean this cat was down on all fours kicking and bucking his hind quarters, arching his neck up and giving it a full “hee-haw!”   So Mayer, not knowing what to do, quietly excused himself. I figured Ladd would join him, but instead, Alan gets down on the floor and starts joining in the with the barnyard routine himself!  So I’m sitting there in shock and I look at Bogie to take a cue, because Bogie was usually quick on his feet in situations like these, but Bogie was just staring off vacantly into the middle distance with some saliva bubbles percolating on his lip.

At least I got the meta nature of the circled letters spelling out "clip." I was expecting tomorrow's puzzle to be the April Fool's Day one (because I ignore dates sometimes due to retirement), so I did not get that this was the "gotcha" trick: the quips were not really quips nor connected to each other. So is that still meta?

I was hoping for a clever but accessible April Fool's Day puzzle. And while the full was ready, I don't think the "prank" was pulled off in the way the writers and editors had hoped. It's like telling someone that they're pocket is untied. It's sort of amusing, but more confusing as to why someone would think it's funny.

Standing up for the rights of all gullible rubes to say that I don't care for April Fool's Day. I have four older brothers; I get enough tricks...and I still always fall for them. That said, this does seem to be an exceedingly gentle trick. I didn't fully get it until I read the column. Alright puzzle overall, light and breezy.

I do several puzzles every day, including Nerdle -- which is like Wordle, except with numbers instead of words. You have to guess the equation of the day. But, today they posted an extra-special edition. For those who love puzzles, try this one: https://www.nerdlegame.com/games/think-nerdle.html

I - like many here - missed the fun and funky April Fool’s puzzle. That said, I also read this (sorry - I forgot attribution), so maybe that’s an explanation? “By the way, April Fools is cancelled today. Keep your jokes to yourself because this whole year has been one already. We tired. Today is March 32nd.”

I’m with whoever said let’s make this March 32. Puzzle was fine for filling in, though I didn’t get the letter placement part til I read the column, and even after that I didn’t see why it was a joke. I know I’m just not good at this. And yup the rouse/roust got me. I would, however, like to sample an apple dowdy, because it sounds interesting.
This one was accessible and a lot of fun for me, and the joke wasn’t mean or hurtful in any way (like so many April Fool’s pranks have become). Thank you both; I hope you team up again soon!

This puzzle was very approachable for me to which I thoroughly enjoyed it! It took crossing entries to solve most of it. I had put it down, went to work and picked it back up after coming home. Some of the entries I literally took a guess and they ended up being right! This should’ve counted as a Monday puzzle and not a Wednesday!
Note to self: if your crossword magically gets published in NYT, don’t read the comments.  You rock.

After the easification of the crosswords recently, I've been looking for more of a challenge. So for the last 3 days, I've been playing Parseword, from the maker of Wordle. It's a good introduction to cryptic crosswords. I went to try to do some Cryptics in the guardian and had no chance at all. So it is serendipitous that this puzzle fell today. It was too easy and belongs on a Monday (I finished Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday in the exact same time this week), and I totally missed the wordplay. I solved through the crosses, and only got the joke after reading the column. However, since I'm getting into Cryptics, I can now appreciate the quips. People, go play parseword! https://www.parseword.com/daily

Huh?

That about sums it up.

@Lewis

Try again—sorry if this shows up twice:
Give me a break! Said the orthopedic surgeon
So what else is gnu? Said the safari guide
And, this is getting very tiring! Said everyone, but in particular the Goodyear employee

@Lewis

Ok 3rd try to post:

Give me a break! Said the orthopedic surgeon
So what else is gnu? Said the safari guide
And, this is getting very tiring! Said everyone, but in particular the Goodyear employee

@Lewis

Give me a break! Said the orthopedic surgeon…
So what else is gnu? Said the safari guide
And, this is getting so tiring… said everyone, but in particular the Goodyear employee.

@SP

Sorry for the triple post. Emus, emus everywhere. Only thing worse that bad puns is having to endure them three times. Mea culpa

@SP

Not at all!  I was initially really impressed that you came up with three sets so quickly.
@SP thanks for your persistence, it was funnier the third time I read it
@SP That's a clever riposte.
@Pagrisan Who does your comment refer to?

@Pagrisan
I’ve thought about that over the years, but have come to believe he wouldn’t mind it. 😇
@Tom  Wow, I initially thought the comment referred to a celebrity but I get it now!
@Pagrisan Cheese and crackers, some people just want to be offended.
@Pagrisan You took offense at that? Geez, Christians are touchy.

@Pagrisan

I'm wondering how your discomfort compares with mine, when, as a member of a local choir, I have to sing about "our Lord, Jesus" even though he ain't MY lord.

@Nick

I had 11 minutes yesterday and 17 today.  (Shrug)
@Charles The circled letter is in the part of the word referenced by the clue.
@Nate S oh goodness, thanks!  I couldn't get it either!

Barry Ancona,
Yes.

@Marshall Walthew
And I don't think he'd be very happy with SOTU being in the puzzle along with his name, given the impending blustering word salad to come tonight.
@Marshall Walthew I didn't get the puzzle at all, but OCHS and OILERS salvaged it for me. Love Phil

@Chris
Search for "shoo fly pie and apple pan dowdy" and you will find the lyrics and performances of this faux-rural song probably written by some cosmopolitan in Manhattan imagining what we now call 'fly-over country'.
Try it. You'll like it! https://www.tasteofhome.com/recipes/apple-pandowdy/
@Steve L 2020 was the best ever!
@Christine - My phone had Q U I P circled.   People have in the past mentioned something about having overlays turned on.
@jennie circles are generally there with or without overlays on. But they can be difficult to see (especially once the grid is filled).

@Joe Horton
I agree, breaking convention is to be applauded on April 1. Also, it would be nice to have a place to discuss the Mini & Midi puzzles, instead of hijacking wordplay like we just did.
@Steven M  256 is pretty good.  Congratulations on reaching a binary milestone.

@Francis
And there may be even more who don't get the joke.
@Francis - I feel hexed by your statement.
@dutchiris if they don’t get the joke, they are in the same category as those who don’t understand binary. 10 is two in binary.

Francis,
I tend to flip a bit: sometimes I’m a ‘binary’ one, and sometimes I’m nought.

@Francis There are two types of people: 

1. Those who can extrapolate from incomplete information

@Francis
the non math corollary is
There are 2 kinds of people in the world:
those who can draw inferences.

@Thomas

Really? I thought the theme was a pretty funny meta-joke about quip themes… ie. the fact that this isn’t one. If anything I thought that the circled letters made it too obvious, but apparently a lot of people missed the theme entirely somehow despite that.
@Mike I’m not saying your puns are tired but, just in case, I’ve installed an early yawning system

@Mike
Too bad you couldn't have turned today's entry into a rhyme.  It would have been more sonar-ous.

@SP

I feel ya.
The constructors are our people.  They work hard (at something they love) to create something for our amusement.  I hate it when commenter criticize them.
But sometimes we feel let down.  And it's not the constructors' fault.  Who to blame?
So, we blame the editors.  In part because we don't really understand their job.  What are they trying to accomplish?  What do they do all day?
But the editors can only work with the puzzles that are submitted.  Do we really believe that they're holding back the "good stuff" that they have on their (metaphorical) shelves in order to annoy us with sub-standard puzzles?
That can't be right, can it?

So, what is going on here?!?

@The X-Phile

If you read the notes, you’ll find that the constructors not only intended this as an April Fools puzzle, but it was a long time in the making. So they’re not blameless this time.

@SP

I wish I could disagree with anything you said here, but I can't.
I've been asking the columnists, who I think of as are liaisons to the editors, to give us an explanation.  (That seemed preferable to just complaining about the dumbing down), but, of course, there has been no reply.

PUZZLE EDITORS AND COLUMNISTS:
Are you there???

@SP

Oops I meant Sam Ezersky, sorry Sam

@Puzzlemucker
Somehow I think Merl Reagle would not be chuckling at this puzzle.

@Times Rita

I liked it.  I have a love/hate relationship with quip puzzles, including Merl's.  I usually start out disliking the format because it can be a bit like an extended Wheel of Fortune, but if cleverly done (which Merl's always were), I would end up enjoying the puzzle.  I tried to make sense of the "quip" in this puzzle as I went along without paying much attention to the circled letters, and was ultimately stymied.  Alas, there was no quip puzzle payoff.  Then I recalled the date, and looked at the circled letters and said Aha.  A subtle April Fool's trick but, indeed, I internally chuckled.

@Becky

Didn’t you read the constructor’s notes? This puzzle was very EELY-quipped.

@Becky
Your passion for eels is puzzling, but I guess that's the nature of amore.

@Becky
No, please, no. I have only begun to recover from the EELILY trauma of 2025. 《twitch》 It took me months to stop seeing EELS all over the puzzles. 《tic》 EELING, EELERS, ELVERS, EELED, EEK!
Maybe I will construct a SARGASSO SEA crossword filled with all sorts of lovely EELS, including RIBBON EELS.
Artemus II launch: I got to see a few seconds of flame as it flew out of the low clouds until it disappeared into the upper clouds. A fair amount brighter than the usual Falcon 9 launches.
@Adin prophecy smh
@Harold - His comment refers to the Mini puzzle.

@jennie

Thank you, Jennie. Mini commenters: PLEASE SAY YOU’RE TALKING ABOUT THE MINI!! It gets so confusing.

@Harold

ARSE isn’t pronounced with a Z sound, needed for the plural of the letter R.
@Caitie Exactly! Thank you! I've been trudging through too many boring complaints... waiting for someone else who actually gets the prank. At least there are a few here who get it!
@Matt 🪺+🐓+🏹

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon

Use your words, Becky.

commentBody:
@A Solver in
But that's the joke, isn't it!
@Sal It's hilarious how many still don't get it

@CB

I think a lot of people get it and are not impressed.

@A Solver in
I guarantee you it's not the worst puzzle in years. In fact it's not even a bad puzzle. It's just unworthy of both a Wednesday and April 1.

@Paul

I wish the last two rhymed so that it could be viewed as the "bizarre children's rhyme" that Sam suggests in the column.
@Elbridge Gerry I was yelling at the TV for her to email nytgates@mytimes.com.

@Elbridge Gerry

I saw that too. She said she contacted NYT to get it reinstated, but there was nothing they could do. Based on what others here have said, that seems odd. I have no personal experience, as I don’t care about streaks. But if I did, I can see how that would be upsetting.

@Thad
lol! me, too!

@Elbridge Gerry

People make up stuff on TV? Wow. I had no idea.

@Elbridge Gerry

Yes, she said she lost her streak when she switched to the app. Caveat emptor.

@Heidi
I have had the app screw up twice, indicating a break in my streak when there wasn’t one. The NYT people reset it both times and apparently had no problem doing so. Don’t know what glitch would have been impossible for them to correct.

@Elbridge Gerry

The best thing about streaks is breaking them, so you realize how insignificant they are.

@Elbridge Gerry

I watched the show but I missed the interviews because I had to attend to something. I don’t know if a 7000-day streak is even possible, since counting streaks started when the proprietary online solve began (as opposed to using Across Lite). 7000 days ago was January 31, 2007, so with the lag between taping and airing the show, that supposed streak had to have started in 2006 at the latest. I think they were still using across Across Lite back then.

@Steve
pretty sure she said 1000, not 7000.

@Laurence of Bessarabia

Could be. Maybe I misheard.

@Scott
Here's the dilemma: If you just say, "I didn't like it," you will be met with replies asking you to explain your opinion. Mere negation really doesn't convey much. On the other hand, provide some whys and wherefores to explain your dislike, and you get a reply like yours. Sigh.

@Scott

What did you like about this? The Monday-level clues or the theme you did not get?

@Scott I totally agree! This is what an April Fools Day prank is supposed to be.

Get someone to fall for something, without suspecting anything, then enjoy when it hits them what day it is. In this case, the constructors can't see us (though the way the world is heading, they might be able to soon!) In any case, I bet if they had seen my dumb face staring at the screen, trying to figure out how the heck this was a quip, they would have fallen on the floor laughing.

Some theories about the complaints:
1: most don't seem to get it yet. They're still annoyed that they don't find the 4-line quip funny or meaningful. Which is hilarious! Just as it was when I didn't get it yet. 😅
2: some might not follow the American April Fools Day tradition in their culture. I just did a quick check and it looks like though many countries have some kind of tradition, some may treat it more like a day to tell funny stories, or play a specific prank, like taping a paper fish to someone's back. Side note: though not linked to a specific date, I admire the Australian drop bear prank, and regret I learned the secret before I could fall victim to the prank!
3: some might just not appreciate being tricked and land on 'ha ha very funny' 

All part of the noble art of April Fools Day!

@CB
You can't leave us hanging like this! What is the Australian drop bear prank? Don't make us have to look it up!

@Andrzej Do you only like things you understand? It's a crossword puzzle, I like solving crossword puzzles, and I solved this one. Unlike many of the commenters here, I don't pitch a fit when the clues don't match whatever arbitrary difficulty rating I'm expecting based on the day of the week. But thanks for the attempted gotcha.

@SBK in TO I would say that in addition to April Fool's, I also detest the current "everyone needs to know my opinion about everything" culture, but I'm participating in it, so maybe I don't hate it that much? I guess I could just...not read these comments. They don't seem to be good for my mental health.

@dutchiris agreed. The theme was weak even after the explanation too — UP FRONT doesn’t have the P in the front, it’s at the end of the first word (ridiculous nit I know but that’s the whole theme apparently so…?)

@Richard

It's PAY UP FRONT, so the P is really up front...

@Andrzej
Couldn't have said it better myself, I read the comments Newest to Oldest, and so far we're in the majority.

@Ιασων

Care to elaborate?
@Steve L it’s a Monday

@Ιασων

Aha. 

I don’t think I’d have liked it any better. 

My theory is that there wasn’t anything better out there this year.
@Steve L 🤣🤣
@MFSTEVE Right? You took that right out of Polish Jerry's mouth! https://en.meming.world/wiki/Polish_Jerry

@Alex H
Hope you will keep submitting!  Clearly you are on to something :)

@CCNY

I generally frown at sentimental stuff but this got to me, in a good way. You're awesome, CC.

@CCNY

That was truly beautiful! Thank you!!!!
@Tim The theme is perfect. It's playing a prank on thousands of crossword puzzle solvers who think they're solving for a funny 4-part quip. It worked!

@CB

I get what you’re saying, but for me, it fell flat. Maybe it’s a newer vs experienced solvers kind of thing.

@Jim in Forest Hills
Totally agree with you, about the quote as well as Phil Ochs. Oh, how we need him now.

@Jim in Forest Hills

Of course, it's not a "quote"; it's a QUIP.
@Jim in Forest Hills Please read the puzzle notes. It's an April Fools Day prank. They got me good too. 'Wha?? I don't get it' I thought. Turns out that was the point! 🤣

@Rosa

One of the climbing gyms I used to go to demonstrated a total disregard for grading routes properly. A route graded as 4c could climb like a 4a or a 6a (using the most common European grading system here). The total unpredictability of the whole thing made me stop going there.

@Joan The theme is that there is no theme. It's a prank. We all expected a pithy 4-line quip but in the end the 4 lines were a red herring, and the 4 parts were just the circled letters spelling 'quip'. I fell for it too! Iloved it!

@The X-Phile

I couldn’t figure out why I wasn’t crazy about the puzzle, but you explained it perfectly. What a great analysis.

@The X-Phile

Very insightful analysis.

@The X-Phile
Exactly

@Hugh

Best or worst? :D
DOWDY more often appears here as def. 1, but you might enjoy def. 3 as in the puzzle. I've enjoyed eating it (in Pennsylvania and elsewhere). https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/dowdy

@Jane Wheelaghan
I had trouble with DOWDY. I think it's a Southern thing? Here in the upper Midwest, we have apple kuchen and the like. We got kuchen out the wazoo.

@Katie

Apple PanDOWDY is primarily Pennsylvania "Dutch" thing.  In the South, we mostly eat "cobbler".

@Jane Wheelaghan

DOWDY has only appeared twice in this millennium. Don't bother.

@Steve L,

Thanks for this link to a column from today. I haven’t explored them yet, but I have it bookmarked. And Deb is in the comments! 🙏🏻
@Esmerelda  Yes, i used to (when i actually went there).  I don’t know if people still do.
@Katie In Québec at least it's McDo.

@Esmerelda When I first learned French, I was just as surprised to hear it called "MacDo." 

Mickey Ds in the US, MacDo everywhere else I've ever been.
@Katie pronounced macdough (not macdoo)

Katie & Esmerelda,
Never heard MacDo’s before.

@Eaga

Macca’s in Australia, I believe. Maybe UK too?

@Steve L

Update:  Someone said they call it Maccie's in the UK.

@Eaga Here's a whole list of nicknames for the Golden Arches (there's another one): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_nicknames_for_McDonald%27s I like calling them "Les Arcs Dorés"; they serve French fries, don't they? Also, when out on the road, looking for a place to stop for lunch, I often ask, "Do you want to go to a world-famous restaurant for lunch?"

@Rich in Atlanta As threatened: First a Tuesday from January 19, 1999 by Kelly Clark. Theme answers in order: WHENTHATEEL BATSANEYE WINKRIGHTBACK DONTBESHY THATSAMORAY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/19/1999&g=56&d=A I think I'll just leave it at that. ....

@CB we get it—it was weak sauce.

Nothing amazing about it, it was meh.
@CB Not just in the US, as evidenced by the old BBC "spaghetti tree" broadcast from decades ago

@Erik
The Guardian newspaper used to do them too, haven't checked lately. They once had an ad for a new holiday resort called San Serif
@Nancy Mickey D’s is McDonald’s

@Nancy
McDonald’s … hardly propaganda for meat substitutes…and there are about 250 in the 5 boroughs.

Nancy,
(1) It's April Fool's Day.
(2) It's Apple pan DOWDY (or Apple Pandowdy). It's been here since Colonial Times. I've had it in Lancaster County PA (and other places).
(3) There are McDonald's in NYC (MICKEYDS).
(4) With you on VEGGIE and SOY.

@Nancy "If memory serves, we all hate this variety of puzzle, don't we?"

We don't all--see my comment below.

But I'm complete agreement with you on "meat substitutes!" Nothing wrong with a good plate of tofu--just don't try to make me believe it's a hamburger!

FWIW, in some countries and locations, Mickey D's will offer vegetarian substitute burgers--the McPlant®, but also, in India the McSpicy Paneer® and the McAloo Tikka®.

The only Mickey D's I eat at regularly is one of I-75, just north of Saginaw, MI--very clean, very friendly. I will have to ask for a McPlant the next time I stop.

Postscript:  Aha!  It's an April Fool puzzle.  The circled letters spell out QUIP.  You definitely fooled me, Jess and Amie.  This gives the puzzle a certain je ne sais quois that it didn't have before.  And while it's far from the sharpest April Fool puzzle trick that's ever been perpetrated on us, it's definitely a trick.  Kudos for your sleight of hand..
@Nancy No chain restaurants in NYC? You obviously (and rightly) avoid Times Square. But c'mon, man!

@Carol SN

(Q)UICK
YO(U)NG
L(I)NE
(P)AY 

Hope that helps. 

...
@Carol SN I didn’t get the “quip” on my own either, but Sam’s column helped!

@Carol SN
I was looking for a deeper meaning, and you may have been too. There no overall four-part quip here. Each of the four theme answers describes the position of the circled letter within the answer. Nancy lays it out in detail very well a few comments below yours. Happy Foolishness Day!

@Garima

I am confused.  Please tell me what this means.

@Jim

It means:

1. Garima is in the wrong comments.
2. Garima did not successfully solve Wordle today.
@Jim - It means Garima did not try *all* the vowels other than Y, even though there were five opportunities to do so.
@Marlene Missed opportunity for an ALOUD pun haha

@Bill

Not Kroc. "McDonald's was founded by brothers Richard ("Dick") and Maurice ("Mac") McDonald in 1940 in San Bernardino, California." - mcdonalds.com

@Elbridge Gerry
Didn’t Kroc buy the name, and franchise it?
@Margaret   Ooh!  Thanks so much for reminding me about the song, which I haven’t heard in…decades? —until just now- June’s version- cool, man, cool.

@Bill. I am not a big fan of the blast of the horns, it just is jarring to me.  I think he was a talented man, but his style wasn't my favorite. I love June Christy though, and of course Dinah Shore is always fun!

@Nancy J.

That would have been a better puzzle, but I think it would have required a revealer clue/answer that hinted at the cryptic themers.

@The X-Phile

No, I think the whole joke would be that there is no easy way to figure it out.  Some people might, (I doubt I would) and the rest of us would eventually be driven to Wordplay to find that we were had.

@Nancy J.
Agreed on the circles. Make we work for it, at least a little bit. This was smack-in-the-face obvious.

@Grant

Right?  We're here for the struggle,

MaryEllen,

You know what they* say:
Give the cats their food, and they’ll eat once.
Teach the cats to do your puzzles for you, and you can feed them forever.

* “they” being the cats, if you teach them to talk.

@Bill
Thanks for this rundown! I had forgotten that pandowdy even existed until today's puzzle and wondered what it was... I guess not enough curiosity to look it up, but certainly enough to enjoy the fruits of your labor. ;-)

Bill,

One I never liked was the solitary, peel-on “Baked Apple”. No dough-boys, no dowdy clothing of any kind. Just a little brown sugar, cinnamon, nutmeg, maybe walnuts on a good day, in a cored and heat-slumped bag of apple slush. My mother loved to make them for us and talk it up as a near-luxury. Maybe a Depression-era treat of her youth, I’m not sure.

@Bill
Back in the days when I was eating MREs, When you got the applesauce dessert (I think it came with the ham slice?) you would mix it with crumbled crackers and a packet of non-dairy creamer and pretend it was apple cobbler. It wasn't terrible. I had no idea what a pandowdy is, despite having lived in the South, but I'd heard of it.

@Bill Grunts actually do kind of grunt when you make them. Grunts are fruit and juice with dumpling bits dropped in and steamed when you put a lid on the pan. I've only made blueberry, and it really needs to be the more flavorful wild/small blueberries.

@DocP If you haven’t already checked it out, The Guardian has been running “cryptics for beginners” articles since 2011.
